channel.title: Ilia Klishin Publications Feed
channel.description: Publication cards with concise summaries and links to original sources.

item.title: Putin-Macron talks and the Russia expertise gap
item.description:
A small factual mistake in coverage of the Putin-Macron meeting exposed a larger expertise gap in foreign commentary on Russia. Minor errors can reveal a shallow analytical frame when they harden into repeated claims.
It connects media analysis with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2022/02/14/putin-macron-talks-highlight-gap-in-russia-expertise-a76375

item.title: Mass protests: lessons from a decade
item.description:
Ten years after Bolotnaya, the 2011 protest wave looks like political learning rather than simple defeat. A generation that entered the streets naively left with a clearer understanding of the state, repression, and civic action.
It offers a concise entry point into the debate around civic protests.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/12/10/russias-mass-protests-10-years-on-finding-hope-in-apparent-defeat-a75781

item.title: The Kremlin Is Coming for Media One by One — and Society Is Helpless to Stop It
item.description:
Pressure against independent media often works through accumulation rather than a single ban. Legal risk, economic pressure, isolation, and exhaustion weaken outlets before formal censorship completes the process.
This piece places media freedom in a concrete editorial moment in 2021.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/08/24/the-kremlin-is-coming-for-media-one-by-one-and-society-is-helpless-to-stop-it-a74879

item.title: The Magic Mirror of Russian Public Opinion
item.description:
After VkusVill published and then deleted its story about two women hoping to marry, both supporters and opponents rushed to speak in the name of “public opinion.”
It connects public opinion with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/07/12/the-magic-mirror-of-public-opinion-a74492

item.title: The Dilemma of the Great Terror: How to Report Roman Protasevich's Confession?
item.description:
Roman Protasevich’s arrest and televised confession in Minsk created a problem of method, not only politics. Journalists had to cover the spectacle without treating coerced speech as ordinary evidence or helping the state turn intimidation into authority.
It offers a concise entry point into the debate around media ethics.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/06/07/the-dilemma-of-the-great-terror-how-to-report-roman-protasevichs-confession-a74125

item.title: Let's Wait Until After the Elections
item.description:
The familiar political refrain “let’s wait until after the elections” works less as caution than as a technology of delay. What sounds prudent often serves to normalize passivity and postpone real choices indefinitely.
It explains electoral timing and links to the original source.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/05/17/lets-wait-until-after-the-elections-a73915

item.title: Dekoder zu Sputnik V, EU-Zulassung und russischer Impfkommunikation
item.description:
Der Dekoder-Artikel behandelt die Debatte um Sputnik V in der EU und zitiert Klishin zur Kommunikationslage in Russland. Er verknüpft die internationale Impfstoffwerbung mit innenpolitischem Misstrauen nach Jahren verschwörerischer Staatsrhetorik.
Referenzeintrag dazu, wie deutschsprachige Berichterstattung Klishins Einordnung in der Sputnik-V-Debatte nutzt.
Original source: https://www.dekoder.org/de/article/sputnik-v-impfstoff-eu-zulassung/

item.title: Dekoder on Sputnik V approval and Russian vaccine messaging
item.description:
Dekoder links Sputnik V promotion abroad to domestic mistrust shaped by years of conspiracy-driven state broadcasting. Vaccine messaging that sounded triumphant outside Russia met an audience trained to doubt official narratives.
Reference card on how German-language coverage used Klishin commentary in the Sputnik V and trust debate.
Original source: https://www.dekoder.org/de/article/sputnik-v-impfstoff-eu-zulassung/

item.title: Dekoder sobre Sputnik V, la UE y la narrativa rusa de vacunacion
item.description:
El articulo vincula la promocion externa de la vacuna con la desconfianza interna acumulada por anos de retorica conspirativa estatal.
Referencia sobre como un medio germanoparlante utilizo comentarios de Klishin en el debate de Sputnik V.
Original source: https://www.dekoder.org/de/article/sputnik-v-impfstoff-eu-zulassung/

item.title: Dekoder sur Sputnik V, l UE et la communication vaccinale russe
item.description:
Il relie la promotion externe du vaccin a une mefiance interne produite par des annees de rhetorique conspirationniste.
Reference utile sur l usage des analyses de Klishin dans un media germanophone au sujet de Sputnik V.
Original source: https://www.dekoder.org/de/article/sputnik-v-impfstoff-eu-zulassung/

item.title: Luiza Rozova and the Helpless Intellectual
item.description:
The public fascination with Luiza Rozova becomes a test of symbolic dissent inside an authoritarian culture. Irony and gossip can expose elite absurdity, but they rarely create accountability on their own.
This piece places elite discourse in a concrete editorial moment in 2021.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/03/19/luiza-rozova-and-the-helpless-intellectual-a73301

item.title: Bloomberg zitiert Klishin zur Twitter-Drosselung in Russland
item.description:
Der Bloomberg-Bericht behandelt Russlands Entscheidung, Twitter zu verlangsamen, als Teil eines breiteren Vorgehens gegen westliche Plattformen. Klishin ist nicht Autor, wird aber zitiert, um den Druckaufbau ohne sofortige Vollsperre zu erklären.
Externe Referenz, die Klishins Einordnung in der internationalen Berichterstattung zur Plattformpolitik in Russland dokumentiert.
Original source: https://www.chinadailyhk.com/hk/article/160056

item.title: Bloomberg quotes Klishin on Russia’s Twitter crackdown
item.description:
Bloomberg used Klishin’s commentary to explain why Russian authorities were increasing pressure on Western platforms without yet moving to a full ban. Twitter throttling appears as a calibrated escalation rather than a one-off technical measure.
External feature card documenting how Bloomberg used Klishin commentary while reporting Russia’s platform pressure strategy.
Original source: https://www.chinadailyhk.com/hk/article/160056

item.title: Bloomberg cita a Klishin sobre la ralentización de Twitter en Rusia
item.description:
Bloomberg cubre la decisión rusa de ralentizar Twitter dentro de una presión más amplia sobre plataformas occidentales. Klishin no es autor del texto, pero aparece citado para explicar una escalada que todavía no llegaba al bloqueo total.
Referencia externa que muestra el uso de comentarios de Klishin en una cobertura internacional sobre política digital rusa.
Original source: https://www.chinadailyhk.com/hk/article/160056

item.title: Bloomberg cite Klishin sur le ralentissement de Twitter en Russie
item.description:
Bloomberg décrit la décision russe de ralentir Twitter dans un cycle plus large de pression sur les plateformes occidentales. Klishin n’en est pas l’auteur; il y est cité pour expliquer une escalade qui restait en deçà d’un blocage total.
Référence externe attestant l’usage de commentaires de Klishin dans une couverture internationale sur la régulation des plateformes.
Original source: https://www.chinadailyhk.com/hk/article/160056

item.title: Four Ways the Kremlin Could Block Western Social Networks in Russia
item.description:
Practical routes for restricting Western platforms in Russia include regulation, technical throttling, legal pressure, and direct demands on companies. A block does not have to arrive as one dramatic act; it can be assembled gradually.
It connects social network regulation with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/02/12/four-ways-the-kremlin-could-block-western-social-networks-in-russia-a72920

item.title: The Capitol Storming Gives Russians an Escape From Their Reality
item.description:
For Russian audiences, the storming of the U.S. Capitol worked less as foreign news than as psychological relief. Attention could be redirected outward, toward Western disorder, and away from a domestic reality that state media preferred to keep emotionally unexamined.
It offers a concise entry point into the debate around comparative media framing.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/01/14/the-capitol-storming-gives-russians-an-escape-from-their-reality-a72598

item.title: Warning! An Army of Russian Trolls Has Invaded TikTok
item.description:
Russian propaganda did not become new on TikTok; it changed packaging. Familiar manipulation techniques were adapted for algorithmic entertainment, youth culture, and faster attention cycles.
It explains disinformation and platforms and links to the original source.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/12/18/warning-an-army-of-russian-trolls-has-invaded-tiktok-a72410

item.title: Why Russians Dislike "Good Russians" in American Films No Less than "Bad Russians"
item.description:
Russian viewers often recognize themselves even in sympathetic American film stereotypes. “Good Russian” portrayals can flatten culture and politics in much the same way as openly hostile ones.
It connects cultural representation with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/11/20/why-russians-dislike-good-russians-in-american-films-no-less-than-bad-russians-a72097

item.title: Great Russian Literature Versus One Tweet by Stephen King
item.description:
A single Stephen King tweet makes the symbolic weight of Russian literature look slow and cumbersome. The contrast opens a wider point about authority, attention, and cultural emotion moving on different timescales.
It offers a concise entry point into the debate around cultural signaling.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/11/09/great-russian-literature-versus-one-tweet-by-stephen-king-a71993

item.title: Anonymous Telegram Channels Are Shaping Russia's Daily Life
item.description:
Anonymous Telegram channels ceased to be just rumor mills once they began shaping tone, agenda, and everyday political perception. The line between leaks, manipulation, gossip, and coordination grows harder to draw the more central these channels become.
It explains platform influence and links to the original source.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/09/28/anonymous-telegram-channels-are-shaping-russias-reality-a71588

item.title: Why Do Russians Resent Western Media Over the Protests in Belarus?
item.description:
Russian resentment toward Western coverage of Belarus grows from projection as much as geopolitics. The argument separates solidarity with protesters from a defensive media reflex that treats outside attention as humiliation.
This piece places belarus coverage framing in a concrete editorial moment in 2020.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/08/14/why-do-russians-resent-western-media-over-the-protests-in-belarus-a71155

item.title: Coronavirus Spoils Putin's 'Forever-in-Power' Public Vote
item.description:
The pandemic disrupted the timing of Putin’s constitutional vote and exposed the fragility of a carefully staged political ritual. What had been designed as controlled acclamation became harder to manage once public health, delay, and legitimacy collided.
It offers a concise entry point into the debate around constitutional vote.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/04/16/coronavirus-spoils-putins-forever-in-power-vote-a70014

item.title: Why Medvedev's resignation and Putin's amendments changed nothing
item.description:
The January 2020 constitutional reshuffle created an image of political movement while leaving the real structure of power untouched. Formal offices mattered less than the single center that controlled them.
This piece places constitutional politics in a concrete editorial moment in 2020.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/01/17/10-2-a68947

item.title: Who owns Russian? And who owns English?
item.description:
Arguments about language become arguments about authority, belonging, and cultural ownership. Russian and English raise the same political question: who gets to define a language from the center, and who is left speaking from the margins.
It connects language politics with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2019/11/11/9-a68123

item.title: Why shoot Navalny if you can bankrupt him
item.description:
Financial pressure becomes a central tool of modern repression. Instead of visible violence, authorities can exhaust opponents through fines, frozen accounts, and legal costs while preserving the appearance of procedural legality.
It offers a concise entry point into the debate around economic repression.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2019/10/30/klishin-why-hold-mass-executions-when-bankruptcies-work-a67972

item.title: How Putin Is Bankrupting the Russian Opposition
item.description:
Financial pressure can function as repression. Fines, frozen accounts, debt, and procedural penalties disable political work without the visible drama of mass arrests, while the appearance of legality becomes part of the mechanism.
It explains economic repression and links to the original source.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2019/10/30/how-putin-is-bankrupting-the-russian-opposition-a67971

item.title: How the death penalty debate linked to a ban on negative Ukraine coverage
item.description:
Two media agendas reveal how distraction campaigns are built. Emotionally loaded topics can be amplified to redirect public attention and normalize selective information boundaries around politically sensitive narratives.
It explains media framing and links to the original source.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2019/10/16/8-2-a67761

item.title: Russia’s New Heroes Are Those Who Simply Say Things as They Are
item.description:
Plain factual speech becomes politically meaningful when institutions distort language and incentives. Minimal honesty from insiders can shift public attention and recalibrate legitimacy precisely because the surrounding system punishes ordinary candor.
This piece places public discourse in a concrete editorial moment in 2019.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2019/09/26/russias-new-heroes-are-those-who-simply-say-things-as-they-are-a67400

item.title: Why Russian Journalists Find Refuge in Propaganda Outlets
item.description:
Compromise appears here as a structural problem, not only a moral failure. When the independent media market contracts, career precarity can push journalists toward propaganda institutions even without ideological conversion.
It connects media labor market with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2019/04/05/why-russian-journalists-find-refuge-in-propaganda-outlets-a65108

item.title: The music of the media desert
item.description:
Shrinking pluralism produces a repetitive and hostile media environment. A system built on noise and intimidation erodes nuance, memory, and professional standards.
This piece places media ecosystem in a concrete editorial moment in 2018.
Original source: https://www.vedomosti.ru/opinion/columns/2018/12/06/788550-muzika-mediinoi-pustini

item.title: Moscow: clean city, cleansed city
item.description:
Urban cleanliness becomes a political metaphor for controlled public space. Aesthetic order can coexist with social exclusion, narrowed civic participation, and managed dissent.
It connects urban politics with the original text published by Vedomosti.
Original source: https://www.vedomosti.ru/opinion/columns/2018/05/24/770522-moskva-chistaya-i-zachischennaya

item.title: From Kremlin Web Brigades to Russian Troll Factories
item.description:
Early online brigades later hardened into industrial-scale troll operations. Klishin’s analysis appears in that broader vocabulary for describing Russian influence infrastructure.
It connects disinformation infrastructure with the original text published by VSquare.
Original source: https://vsquare.org/from-kremlin-web-brigades-to-russian-troll-factories/

item.title: Freedom on the Net 2016 erfasst Druckmuster im Umfeld von Klishin und Kollegen
item.description:
Das Russland-Kapitel von Freedom House 2016 dokumentiert digitalen Druck und Angriffe auf Konten unabhängiger Akteure.
Institutionelle Quelle zur Einordnung digitaler Einschüchterung gegen unabhängige Medien- und Zivilakteure.
Original source: https://freedomhouse.org/country/russia/freedom-net/2016

item.title: Freedom on the Net 2016 records pressure linked to Klishin and peers
item.description:
Freedom House's 2016 Russia chapter documents cyber pressure and account targeting against independent actors and investigators. Separate incidents read as part of a wider timeline of digital intimidation and online-control practices.
Institutional source supporting chronology and context for digital intimidation patterns around independent media and activists.
Original source: https://freedomhouse.org/country/russia/freedom-net/2016

item.title: Freedom on the Net 2016 registra presion digital vinculada a Klishin y su entorno
item.description:
El capitulo sobre Rusia de Freedom House en 2016 documenta presion digital y ataques contra cuentas de actores independientes. Esta entrada se incluye como registro institucional dentro de la cronologia de intimidacion y control de internet.
Fuente institucional para contextualizar patrones de intimidacion digital contra actores civicos y periodisticos.
Original source: https://freedomhouse.org/country/russia/freedom-net/2016

item.title: Freedom on the Net 2016 documente des pressions liees a Klishin et ses pairs
item.description:
Le chapitre Russie de Freedom House en 2016 documente des pressions numeriques et des attaques contre des comptes d acteurs independants. Cette entree conserve une source institutionnelle dans la chronologie des pratiques d intimidation et de controle en ligne.
Source institutionnelle utile pour documenter le contexte des intimidations numeriques visant acteurs civiques et medatiques.
Original source: https://freedomhouse.org/country/russia/freedom-net/2016

item.title: Private Authoritarianism: How Facebook and Google Threaten Democracy
item.description:
Major private platforms gained political power without democratic accountability. Algorithmic ranking, moderation policy, and opaque product decisions can shape public debate as forcefully as state media controls, making platform governance a political problem rather than only a technical or market issue.
Strengthens the archive with an early argument about platform governance, accountability, and democratic risk beyond state media frameworks.
Original source: https://carnegieendowment.org/ru/russia-eurasia/posts/2016/09/chastnyj-avtoritarizm-kak-facebook-i-google-ugrozhayut-demokratii

item.title: Web of Control: The Future of the Russian Internet
item.description:
State control over the Russian internet advances through policy, platform pressure, and institutional coordination. Once-unthinkable restrictions become routine options in public governance.
It offers a concise entry point into the debate around internet governance.
Original source: https://carnegieendowment.org/posts/2016/01/web-of-control-the-future-of-the-russian-internet

item.title: Gazeta.ru-Interview zur möglichen Sperre von YouTube und Google
item.description:
Ru-Interview dokumentiert einen Expertenkommentar zu technischen und politischen Szenarien einer Plattformsperre in Russland. Klishin wird zu möglichen Folgen einer Einschränkung von YouTube oder Google sowie zur breiteren Logik digitaler Kontrolle zitiert.
Referenzeintrag zur frühen Berichterstattung über Plattformsperren und digitale Kontrolle in Russland.
Original source: https://www.gazeta.ru/tech/news/2016/01/16/n_8127887.shtml

item.title: Gazeta.ru interview on possible blocking of YouTube and Google
item.description:
Blocking major platforms in Russia was already both a technical scenario and a political signal. Restricting YouTube or Google would test how far the state was prepared to push digital control.
Reference entry for early media discussion of platform blocking risks in Russia.
Original source: https://www.gazeta.ru/tech/news/2016/01/16/n_8127887.shtml

item.title: N 8127887.shtml
item.description:
Esta entrevista de Gazeta. ru recoge un comentario experto sobre escenarios tecnicos y politicos de bloqueo de grandes plataformas en Rusia.
Entrada de referencia sobre debate temprano en medios acerca de bloqueo de plataformas en Rusia.
Original source: https://www.gazeta.ru/tech/news/2016/01/16/n_8127887.shtml

item.title: Entretien Gazeta.ru sur un possible blocage de YouTube et Google
item.description:
Cet entretien de Gazeta. ru conserve un commentaire expert sur les scenarios techniques et politiques de blocage des grandes plateformes en Russie.
Entree de reference sur les discussions mediales precoces autour des risques de blocage de plateformes en Russie.
Original source: https://www.gazeta.ru/tech/news/2016/01/16/n_8127887.shtml

item.title: JSIS-Profil: digitaler Patriotismus und nationalistische Agenda in russischen Medien
item.description:
Dieses JSIS-Profil untersucht digitalen Patriotismus und nationalistisches Narrativmanagement in der russischen Medienpolitik. Es dient als institutionelle Kontextquelle zu Rechtsunschärfe, Zensurpraktiken und Bezügen zu Klishins Medienanalyse.
Referenzeintrag zur Verknüpfung akademischer Politikforschung und Klishin-bezogenem Medienkontext.
Original source: https://jsis.washington.edu/news/russia-media-profile-digital-patriotism-nationalist-agenda/

item.title: JSIS profile: digital patriotism and nationalist agenda in Russian media
item.description:
Digital patriotism appears not as a mood, but as a managed media strategy. Nationalism, legal ambiguity, and information control operate as parts of the same political framework.
Reference entry linking academic policy analysis of Russian media controls to Klishin-related analytical context.
Original source: https://jsis.washington.edu/news/russia-media-profile-digital-patriotism-nationalist-agenda/

item.title: Perfil JSIS: patriotismo digital y agenda nacionalista en medios rusos
item.description:
Este perfil de JSIS estudia el patriotismo digital y la gestion de narrativas nacionalistas en la politica mediatica rusa. Se incluye como fuente institucional sobre ambiguedad legal, practicas de censura y contexto analitico asociado a Klishin.
Entrada de referencia que conecta analisis academico de control mediatico ruso con contexto analitico ligado a Klishin.
Original source: https://jsis.washington.edu/news/russia-media-profile-digital-patriotism-nationalist-agenda/

item.title: Profil JSIS : patriotisme numerique et agenda nationaliste dans les medias russes
item.description:
Ce profil JSIS analyse le patriotisme numerique et la gestion des recits nationalistes dans la politique mediatique russe.
Entree de reference reliant analyse academique des controles mediatique russes et contexte analytique lie a Klishin.
Original source: https://jsis.washington.edu/news/russia-media-profile-digital-patriotism-nationalist-agenda/

item.title: Do Revolutions Grow Out of Social Media?
item.description:
Social networks can accelerate visibility, coordination, and emotional contagion — but they do not produce revolutions on their own. Post-Soviet examples separate communication effects from the political structures that make mass mobilization possible.
Useful for separating media amplification from political causality in debates about digital protest dynamics across post-Soviet contexts.
Original source: https://carnegieendowment.org/ru/posts/2015/10/samizdat-i-zameshenie-rastut-li-iz-socsetej-revolyucii

item.title: Bot Army: Why the Kremlin Fails at Soft Power
item.description:
Kremlin bot activity looked less like successful soft power than a domestic political tactic built around noise, intimidation, and simulated consensus. Russian online influence appears here as infrastructure, not just messaging.
Dated Carnegie source for Klishin’s critique of Kremlin bot networks and failed soft power.
Original source: https://carnegie.ru/commentary/60728

item.title: What Sparked Moldova's Second Maidan?
item.description:
In Moldova, an apparent political breakthrough did not end the frustrations that had produced protest in the first place. Repeated cycles of disappointment between reform promises and lived reality reopened the street as a political arena.
Adds a comparative regional case on protest recurrence, useful for interpreting delayed political effects after apparent electoral wins.
Original source: https://carnegieendowment.org/ru/posts/2015/05/protiv-chego-povtorno-sobiralsya-moldavskij-majdan
